questions.question: Which prefix identifies an IPv6 link-local address?
questions.explanation: Link-local addresses are automatically generated in FE80::/10 (always start with FE8) and are used only on the local link.

questions.question: What range is used for IPv6 multicast addresses?
questions.explanation: IPv6 multicast addresses are in FF00::/8 — one-to-many addressing. IPv6 has no broadcast; multicast replaces it.

questions.question: What is an IPv6 anycast address?
options: One-to-one-of-many — multiple devices share an address and traffic is routed to the nearest one
One-to-all — sent to every host on the local link
One-to-many — sent to every subscribed group member
questions.explanation: Anycast is one-to-one-of-many: multiple devices share the same address and traffic is routed to the nearest one.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 loopback address?
questions.explanation: The IPv6 loopback address is ::1 — the IPv6 equivalent of 127.0.0.1 (a single address instead of a whole block).

questions.question: What is the unspecified address in IPv6?
options: ::  (all 0s) — used when a device doesn't know its own address; also the IPv6 default route (::/0)
questions.explanation: The unspecified address is :: (all 0s), used when a device doesn't know its own address; ::/0 is also the IPv6 default route.

questions.question: Which IPv6 multicast address reaches all routers on the local link?
questions.explanation: FF02::2 is the all-routers link-local multicast. FF02::1 is all-nodes; FF02::5/::6 are OSPF; FF02::A is EIGRP.

questions.question: What does NDP stand for, and what does it replace?
options: Neighbor Discovery Protocol; IPv6's replacement for ARP
Network Discovery Protocol; replaces DHCP for IPv6
Node Delivery Protocol; replaces ICMP in IPv6
Neighbor Datagram Protocol; replaces UDP for link-local traffic
questions.explanation: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) is IPv6's replacement for ARP and uses link-local addresses.

questions.question: Which prefix identifies a unique local address (ULA) in IPv6?
questions.explanation: A Unique Local Address (ULA) is a private IPv6 address in FC00::/7 — in practice always begins with FD. ULAs are not routed on the Internet.

questions.question: Which IPv6 address range was originally used for global unicast addresses?
questions.explanation: Global unicast addresses — public, globally routable — were originally assigned from 2000::/3.

questions.question: Which multicast scope keeps traffic inside the local subnet?
questions.explanation: Link-local scope (FF02) keeps multicast traffic inside the local subnet. FF01 is interface-local (stays in the device); FF05 is site; FF0E is global.

questions.question: Which multicast address is used by OSPF all-routers in IPv6?
questions.explanation: FF02::5 is the OSPF all-routers multicast (FF02::6 is OSPF all-DR-routers). FF02::A is for EIGRP routers.

questions.question: What is the Global ID in a unique local address?
options: 40-bit randomly generated portion of a ULA (after FD)
The leading /48 assigned by an ISP
questions.explanation: The Global ID is the 40-bit randomly generated portion of a unique local address, placed right after the FD prefix.

questions.question: Which command configures an anycast address on an interface?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 address <addr>/128 anycast` configures an anycast address on an interface.
